questions.stem: What does the Actualization phase consist of, according to the lab definition?

options.text: Locating the concept, analysing the impact set, and prefactoring the code
Initiation of the change request, verification, and conclusion
Implementation of the new functionality, its incorporation into the old code, and change propagation
Postfactoring the new code, testing it, and releasing it

questions.explanation: The lab defines actualization as implement + incorporate + propagate: 'the implementation of the new functionality, its incorporation into the old code, and change propagation that seeks out and updates all places in the old code that require secondary modification.' A lists earlier phases (Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring). B lists the surrounding process steps, not actualization's content. D describes later/parallel activities. [ActLab]

questions.stem: How are SMALL changes handled during actualization, per the deck?

options.text: Implemented only after a full impact-set re-analysis
Written as new classes in isolation and then incorporated
Edited directly into the existing old code
Deferred to the Postfactoring phase

questions.explanation: Small changes are 'done directly in the old code' — the deck's example is widening a ZIP field inside the existing Address class. B describes the technique for LARGER changes. D and A invent steps not tied to change size. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: Why does the deck recommend writing the new classes SEPARATELY from the old code before incorporating them?

options.text: Because separate classes are exempt from change propagation
So they can be made correct and testable in isolation before wiring them in, after which incorporation begins the ripple
Because the compiler forbids editing two files in the same change
So the impact set can be recomputed from scratch each time

questions.explanation: Building new classes apart from old code lets you get them right and testable before connecting them; only at incorporation do ripple effects begin. C is a fabricated compiler rule. D confuses this with Impact Analysis. A is false — incorporated classes are exactly what triggers propagation. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: A team adds a new animal type by inserting 'else if (type==PIG)' branches into Farm's existing methods instead of subclassing. Why does this contradict the deck's polymorphic-extension lesson?

options.text: It improves recall of the impact analysis automatically
It edits the clients (Farm's code) rather than adding a leaf subclass, so the change ripples and violates Open/Closed
It is a valid small change and matches the ZIP example exactly
It is required because C++ cannot dispatch dynamically

questions.explanation: The deck's point is that polymorphism extends behaviour by adding a subclass while leaving Farm untouched; editing Farm with type conditionals modifies the existing client, makes the change ripple, and violates Open/Closed. C mischaracterises a structural change as the trivial ZIP edit; D is false (D++ supports virtual dispatch, which the example uses); A is unrelated. (Actualization p.6-7; OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: The deck summarises the Ericsson recall result as the programmers estimating that the change would impact roughly what fraction of classes that actually changed?

options.text: All of them — they missed none
About two thirds — they missed the other third
About a third — they missed the other two thirds
None of them — they missed all

questions.explanation: The deck states programmers 'estimated that the changes will impact only about a third of all classes that actually changed — missed the other two thirds!' (32% recall). The other fractions contradict the 32% figure. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: Which confusion-matrix category contains the DANGEROUS misses in impact analysis?

options.text: True positives (predicted changed and changed)
False positives (predicted changed but unchanged)
False negatives (predicted unchanged but actually changed)
True negatives (predicted unchanged and unchanged)

questions.explanation: False negatives are the dangerous ones — classes you thought were safe but that actually changed; they live in the recall denominator and blow schedules. False positives are merely false alarms; TP and TN are correct predictions. (Actualization p.25-27)

questions.stem: Why is impact routinely UNDER-estimated, according to the deck?

options.text: Because precision is always low
It is a consequence of invisibility — you cannot see all dependencies
Because programmers deliberately inflate estimates
Because the database is a detail

questions.explanation: Under-estimation is 'a consequence of invisibility' — hidden dependencies you cannot see — which makes planning difficult. C is the opposite behaviour, A is false (precision was 100%), and D is an unrelated Clean Architecture point. (Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: According to the deck, how widespread is under-estimation of impact?

options.text: A sign that recall was 100%
Caused by writing business rules in stored procedures
Common in software engineering and in other fields, and it makes planning difficult
Unique to the Ericsson study and never seen elsewhere

questions.explanation: The deck says under-estimation is 'common in software engineering, a consequence of invisibility', 'makes planning difficult', and is 'common in other fields also.' D contradicts that, A is false, and B is an unrelated Clean Architecture issue. (Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: The exam takeaway from the Ericsson study is that which metric is the HARD one to achieve, and what is the cost of doing it poorly?

options.text: Recall is hard; the cost of low recall is blown schedules (missed classes)
Accuracy is hard; the cost is too many true negatives
Specificity is hard; the cost is too many false alarms
Precision is hard; the cost of low precision is missed classes

questions.explanation: High precision is easy (Ericsson got 100%); high recall is hard (only 32%), and the cost of low recall is the missed classes that blow schedules. D inverts which metric is hard, and B/C name metrics not emphasised by the study. (Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: What does a 'software architecture' refer to, per the Clean Architecture deck?

options.text: A re-usable form of a solution to a local design problem
A class with a single reason to change
The high-level structures of a software system and the discipline of creating such structures
An interface implemented by an interactor

questions.explanation: The deck defines software architecture as 'the high-level structures of a software system and the discipline of creating such structures and systems.' A is the definition of a design pattern, B is SRP, and D is a boundary. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: What does 'independent of the UI' mean as a characteristic of clean architecture?

options.text: The UI must be tested exhaustively
The UI can change easily without changing other system components
The UI is the innermost layer
The UI contains all the business rules

questions.explanation: Independent of the UI means 'UI can change easily, without changing other system components.' D contradicts the layering, A contradicts the 'View so stupid you don't test it' idea, and C is wrong — the UI is outermost. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: Why does keeping the architecture testable, UI-independent, DB-independent and framework-independent make ACTUALIZATION cheaper?

options.text: Because it forces every change to be a small ZIP-style edit
Because it deletes obsolete classes automatically
Because it removes the need for Impact Analysis entirely
Because each independence bounds how far a corresponding change request can ripple, keeping change propagation short

questions.explanation: The independences bound the ripple of each change category, so propagation stays short and actualization is cheap — the architectural reason for low maintenance cost. C is false (Impact Analysis is still needed), A is false, and B is unrelated. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: Why must source-code dependencies point INWARD in the Clean Architecture?

options.text: Because the database must be at the center
Inner layers are stable abstractions and outer layers are volatile details, so pointing inward makes details depend on abstractions, never the reverse
Because inner layers change most often and need protection
Because the UI is the most stable component

questions.explanation: Inner = stable abstractions, outer = volatile details; pointing inward means details depend on abstractions (the Dependency Inversion Principle at architecture scale). C inverts stability, and A/D contradict the layering. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: A developer makes the Entities layer import a class from the Frameworks & Drivers layer. Why does this violate Clean Architecture?

options.text: It points a source-code dependency outward, breaking the Dependency Rule that inner layers must know nothing about outer layers
It points a dependency inward, which is forbidden
It is allowed because Entities are the most volatile layer
It is fine as long as it compiles

questions.explanation: Entities (inner) importing from Frameworks & Drivers (outer) points a dependency outward, violating the Dependency Rule — inner layers must know nothing about outer layers. B mislabels the direction, C inverts volatility, and D ignores the rule. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: What is a Boundary in the Clean Architecture data flow?

options.text: A View that displays strings
A concrete database connection class
The innermost entity object
An interface/protocol through which data enters and leaves the interactor

questions.explanation: A Boundary is an interface/protocol: data comes into interactors through a boundary implemented by the interactor, and goes out to boundaries implemented by other objects. B, C, and A are concrete/other components, not the boundary interface. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: Who introduced the theory of the SOLID principles, and in which paper?

options.text: Craig Larman, in 'Applying UML and Patterns'
Martin Fowler, in 'Refactoring'
Michael Feathers, in 'Working Effectively with Legacy Code'
Robert C. Martin, in his 2000 paper 'Design Principles and Design Patterns'

questions.explanation: The SOLID theory was introduced by Robert C. Martin in his 2000 paper 'Design Principles and Design Patterns.' Larman is GRASP, Feathers coined the SOLID acronym (but didn't introduce the theory), and Fowler wrote Refactoring. (OOPrinciples p.2)

questions.stem: In the SRP deck example, which class violates SRP by both changing passwords AND verifying access?

options.text: LoanApprovalHandler
SecurityService
UserService (it both changePassword and checkAccess)
Payments

questions.explanation: The 'without S' UserService both changes passwords and verifies access (checkAccess) — two reasons to change. SecurityService is the EXTRACTED class in the fixed version; LoanApprovalHandler is the OCP example, and Payments is the DIP example. (OOPrinciples p.5)

questions.stem: How is DIP the same idea as the Clean Architecture Dependency Rule, and how does the Entity Gateway relate?

options.text: Both make details depend on abstractions with dependencies pointing toward the stable abstraction; the Entity Gateway is DIP made architectural
DIP is a GRASP pattern, not related to architecture
The Entity Gateway violates DIP by depending on the database
DIP points dependencies outward; the Dependency Rule points them inward, so they conflict

questions.explanation: DIP at class scale and the Dependency Rule at architecture scale both make details depend on abstractions, with dependencies pointing toward the stable abstraction; the Entity Gateway is DIP made architectural. D claims a false conflict, C is wrong, and B miscategorises DIP. (OOPrinciples p.12; Clean Architecture p.8, p.18)

questions.stem: Per the CRP slide, what is described as a 'catastrophic mistake' in OO systems?

options.text: Using composition as the primary reuse mechanism
Injecting dependencies through constructors
Using inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism
Using interfaces to decouple classes

questions.explanation: The slide calls 'using inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism' one of the most catastrophic mistakes. Composition (A), interfaces (D), and constructor injection (B) are all good practices the deck endorses. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Pure Fabrication's PersistentStorage example corresponds to which Clean Architecture idea?

options.text: The Request Model — packing primitive input
The Gateway/Repository — keeping persistence out of domain entities
The Dependency Rule arrow pointing outward
The View Model — holding display strings

questions.explanation: A made-up PersistentStorage class keeps persistence out of domain entities — exactly the Clean Architecture Gateway/Repository idea. The View Model and Request Model are data-flow structures, and the Dependency Rule points inward, not outward. (OOPrinciples p.26; Clean Architecture p.7, p.18)

questions.stem: What is JHotDraw, the course's standard case study?

options.text: A Java GUI framework for structured drawing editors
A confusion-matrix calculator
A relational database engine
A version-control system

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java GUI framework for structured drawing editors, used to ground every change-process phase. The other options are unrelated tools. (JHotDraw Connection; [ActLab])

questions.stem: What does the Actualization lab require you to provide regarding SOLID and Clean Architecture?

options.text: Examples of the SOLID principles and Clean Architecture in the context of the CASE study
A migration of JHotDraw to NoSQL
A rewrite of JHotDraw in another language
A proof that JHotDraw has no technical debt

questions.explanation: The lab requires examples of the SOLID principles and Clean Architecture IN CONTEXT OF the CASE study. The other options are not lab deliverables. [ActLab]

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, which hierarchy is the textbook OCP/GRASP-Polymorphism case?

options.text: The Figure hierarchy (RectangleFigure, EllipseFigure, LineFigure extending/implementing Figure)
The confusion-matrix categories
The Cashiers/Store/Inventory model
The Bird/Ostrich hierarchy

questions.explanation: JHotDraw's Figure hierarchy (RectangleFigure, EllipseFigure, LineFigure on a Figure abstraction) is the textbook OCP/GRASP-Polymorphism case: a new figure type is new code plugged in with clients untouched. The other options are from different examples. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw mapped onto Clean Architecture, which classes play the Entities/Use-Cases role?

options.text: DrawingView and the Swing components
The HTTP client and database drivers
The drawing model (Drawing, Figure, Handle) — application-independent and application-specific business rules
The Presenter and View Model

questions.explanation: The drawing model (Drawing, Figure, Handle) plays the Entities/Use-Cases role, while DrawingView, Swing components, and tools play the Interface Adapters / Frameworks & Drivers role. A and B are the outer layers, and D are data-flow adapters. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: When actualizing a JHotDraw change like 'add a new tool', how do you score your Impact-Analysis prediction afterward?

options.text: Build a confusion matrix of predicted vs actually-touched Figure/Tool/View classes and compute precision and recall
Count the number of GRASP patterns used
Check whether the View needed testing
Measure how many stored procedures changed

questions.explanation: After propagation, you score the prediction by building a confusion matrix of predicted vs actually-touched classes and computing precision/recall — the Ericsson method applied to the case study. The other options are not the scoring method. (JHotDraw Connection; Actualization p.24-27)

questions.stem: In a clean architecture, where should the new login SCREEN changes be confined during the PoS walkthrough?

options.text: The Request Model primitives
The Entity Gateway interface declaration
The innermost Entities layer
The outer Frameworks & Drivers / View layer only

questions.explanation: If the architecture is clean, the login-screen changes are confined to the outer Frameworks & Drivers / View layer. The entities, the gateway interface, and the request model are insulated from UI-look changes. (Actualization p.12-14; Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: What is the correct order of the seven-phase change process?

options.text: Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Initiation, Impact Analysis, Concept Location, Actualization, Prefactoring, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Initiation, Prefactoring, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Concept Location, Initiation, Impact Analysis, Actualization, Prefactoring, Conclusion, Postfactoring

questions.explanation: The order is Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Conclusion, all under Verification. D, B, and C swap the order of Concept Location, Impact Analysis, or Prefactoring. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Which continuous activity wraps the entire change process?

options.text: Verification
Propagation
Incorporation
Concept Location

questions.explanation: Verification is the continuous activity wrapping all seven phases. Incorporation and propagation are parts of actualization, and Concept Location is a single early phase. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Which set lists the four characteristics of clean architecture together with the correct count of SOLID principles and GRASP patterns?

options.text: Independent of UI/DB only; 7 SOLID; 7 GRASP
Fast + small + documented + tested; 5 SOLID; 5 GRASP
Testable + independent of UI/DB/frameworks; 5 SOLID; 9 GRASP
Testable + independent of UI/DB/frameworks; 9 SOLID; 5 GRASP

questions.explanation: Clean architecture is testable and independent of UI/DB/frameworks; SOLID has 5 principles and GRASP has 9 patterns. D swaps the counts, B lists wrong characteristics, and A miscounts both. (Clean Architecture p.3; OOPrinciples p.3, p.18)

questions.stem: How do SOLID and Clean Architecture make actualization cheaper, in one sentence?

options.text: They lower coupling and put variation behind stable interfaces, so incorporating new code ripples less, propagation terminates sooner, and impact-analysis recall improves
They remove the need for testing and verification
They move all business rules into the database
They guarantee every change is a one-field edit

questions.explanation: SOLID/Clean Architecture lower coupling and wrap variation behind stable interfaces (OCP/DIP/Protected Variations, Dependency Rule), so new code ripples less, propagation ends sooner, and recall improves. D, B, and C contradict the lecture. (Exam Focus)

questions.stem: What is the Repository pattern called in the Clean Architecture deck's Entity Gateway discussion?

options.text: The Delivery Mechanism
The Request Model
The Entity Gateway (interface declared with business rules, implemented in the detail layer)
The Presenter

questions.explanation: The Repository is the Entity Gateway: an interface declared with the business rules and implemented in the detail layer to reach the Database API. The Presenter, Request Model, and Delivery Mechanism are data-flow components. (Clean Architecture p.7, p.18)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five SOLID principles?

options.text: Law of Demeter
Single Responsibility Principle
Open/Closed Principle
Dependency Inversion Principle

questions.explanation: SOLID = SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP. The Law of Demeter (Principle of Least Knowledge) is a separate supporting principle. (OOPrinciples p.3, p.15)

questions.stem: One activity runs continuously, wrapping all seven phases of the change process from start to finish. Which activity is it?

options.text: Concept Location
Verification
Refactoring
Conclusion

questions.explanation: Verification is the continuous activity wrapping all seven phases. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Actualization is the implementation phase of which overall process?

options.text: The testing process
The compilation process
The software change process
The release process

questions.explanation: Actualization is the implementation phase of the software change process. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: The deck's small-change example widens a ZIP field from how many digits to how many?

options.text: 5 to 9
3 to 5
9 to 12
5 to 7

questions.explanation: The Address ZIP field is widened from zip[5] to zip[9]. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: A developer widens a single Address field from a 5-digit to a 9-digit ZIP, touching only that one class and adding no new structure. Which actualization technique is in play?

options.text: Writing a new class separately and incorporating it as a supplier
Polymorphic extension of a composite responsibility via a new subclass
A small change edited directly into the existing old code
Replacement of an obsolete class and rerouting all references

questions.explanation: A localized one-field edit with no new structure is the deck's canonical SMALL change, done directly in old code. The other options describe larger-change shapes. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: A maintainer adds a new TriangleFigure to JHotDraw by writing a class that extends the Figure abstraction; DrawingView and DrawingEditor are untouched. Which principle does this exemplify?

options.text: Interface Segregation Principle
Open/Closed Principle (with GRASP Polymorphism)
Single Responsibility Principle
Law of Demeter

questions.explanation: Adding a Figure subclass without editing clients is the textbook OCP / GRASP-Polymorphism case in JHotDraw. (Actualization p.6-7; OOPrinciples p.6-7)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson result, how did precision and recall compare?

options.text: Both were perfect at 100%
Precision was low (32%) while recall was perfect (100%)
Precision was perfect (100%) while recall was low (32%)
Both were low at 32%

questions.explanation: Ericsson achieved 100% precision (no false alarms) but only 32% recall (missed two-thirds of changed classes). (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: What is the practical meaning difference between high precision and high recall for an impact prediction?

options.text: High precision means no missed classes; high recall means no false alarms
Both mean the same thing
High recall means the prediction was small
High precision means no false alarms; high recall means no missed classes

questions.explanation: Precision = no false alarms (everything flagged really changed); recall = no misses (everything that changed was flagged). (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Just before Actualization, one phase readies a clean insertion point for the new code. Which phase plays that setup role?

options.text: Prefactoring
Postfactoring
Conclusion
Concept Location

questions.explanation: Prefactoring precedes Actualization and prepares a clean place to plug the new code in; Postfactoring follows. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: A change request adds an entirely new authentication responsibility that did not exist before. A programmer wants to write and test it cleanly before wiring it in. Which strategy matches the deck?

options.text: Edit the launch code in place because it is a one-line guard
Write the new class separately, then incorporate it into the existing code
Delete the old launch class and let propagation rebuild it
Add an if/else branch to every existing client class

questions.explanation: A brand-new responsibility is a LARGER change: implement the new class separately, then plug it in (incorporation). Building it in isolation lets you get it right and testable first. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: During Actualization, the predicted impact set from Impact Analysis corresponds to what?

options.text: The classes that can never change
The Entities layer only
The set of classes you now expect to touch during incorporation and propagation
The deleted classes

questions.explanation: The predicted impact set is exactly the classes expected to be touched during incorporation and propagation. (Actualization p.1, p.23)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT listed as a design PATTERN in the Clean Architecture deck?

options.text: Onion
Singleton
Observer
MVC

questions.explanation: Onion is an architecture, not a design pattern. The deck's pattern examples are Singleton, Observer, MVC, MVVM, MVP. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: After plugging a new Cashiers class into the Point-of-Sale launch path, the old 'anyone can launch' code is now inconsistent. What must the programmer do next?

options.text: Stop, since incorporation completes the actualization phase
Re-run Concept Location to find the starting class again
Immediately delete the Store and Inventory classes
Walk the dependency graph updating every inconsistent dependent until consistency is restored

questions.explanation: Incorporation makes dependents inconsistent; change propagation then visits and updates each one until no inconsistent class remains. (Actualization p.16-21)

questions.stem: A product owner requests switching the persistence store from a relational DB to NoSQL. In a Clean Architecture, where is this change actualized?

options.text: Entirely in the Entity Gateway Implementation in the detail layer
In the Entities layer, since they own the data
In the Use Cases / interactors, which control the data
Spread across all four layers equally

questions.explanation: Because the DB is a plug-in behind a gateway interface, the swap is confined to the Entity Gateway Implementation; propagation cannot reach entities or use cases. (Clean Architecture p.16-18)

questions.stem: Mapping JHotDraw onto Clean Architecture, the drawing model (Drawing, Figure, Handle) plays which role?

options.text: Entities / Use-Cases (the business rules)
Frameworks & Drivers
Interface Adapters only
The Delivery Mechanism

questions.explanation: The drawing model holds the application rules, playing the Entities/Use-Cases role; the Swing GUI plays the outer layers. (Clean Architecture p.5-8 applied)

questions.stem: Given a prediction that flagged every changed class but also flagged many unchanged ones, which metric is degraded?

options.text: Recall is low
Precision is low (many false positives)
Both are perfect
Neither metric is affected

questions.explanation: Flagging unchanged classes creates false positives, which lowers precision = TP/(TP+FP). Recall stays high since all changed classes were caught. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: Why does the Actualization lab make the JHotDraw/CASE-study link mandatory for SOLID and Clean Architecture?

options.text: To test memorisation of slide page numbers
Because JHotDraw replaces the change process
Because the lab forbids using JHotDraw
Because the portfolio deliverable requires concrete CASE-study examples of each SOLID principle and of Clean Architecture layering

questions.explanation: The lab requires SOLID examples and a Clean Architecture explanation in the context of the CASE study as a portfolio deliverable. (ActLab p.1)

questions.stem: How does the Presenter differ from the View in the Clean Architecture data flow?

options.text: The Presenter just displays data; the View translates the response model
Both translate and display
Neither touches the Response Model
The Presenter translates the Response Model into a View Model (strings/flags); the View only displays the View Model

questions.explanation: The Presenter (MVP) translates the Response Model into a View Model of display-ready strings/flags; the View just shows it. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: Two teams predict the same change. Team A: TP=20, FP=0, FN=30. Team B: TP=20, FP=30, FN=0. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Team A has better recall than Team B
Both teams have identical precision and recall
Team B missed more changed classes than Team A
Team A matches the Ericsson profile (perfect precision, low recall); Team B has perfect recall but poor precision

questions.explanation: A: precision=100%, recall=40%; B: precision=40%, recall=100%. A mirrors Ericsson's no-false-alarms/many-misses profile. (Actualization p.26-27)
